item.title: This is great! +1
item.content:encoded:
I loved every bit of it. The technical description, the logs every bit of it. Just hope maybe there will update that continues this SCP and, well, take!
Good job, this should have way more upvotes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely not a place where I expected to have this discussion. ^_^ And I admit to having an opinion on the subject.

But this is actually an excellent illustration of how the end run that IUPAC took around the earth sciences actually ends up causing more confusion that it needs to. The question is one of geohistorical dates vs. spans of time. It's subtle distinction, but a necessary one for geology and other earth sciences. And for the last 40 years or so, the convention among those sciences was pretty simple…Ma for geohistorical dates and myr (sometimes m.y.) for spans of time. So 3,000 Ma would basically translate to 3,000,000,000 BCE, a specific time on the calendar (which is how I'm using it here), but if you wanted to talk about a quantity of time you'd say 3,000 myr (ago.)

But then in 2011 the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry and the International Union of Geological Sciences formed a joint task force regarding an SI convention for a year, and, without getting sufficient consensus from the earth sciences and over quite a few (in my opinion extremely valid) objections, adopted 'a' as the abbreviation for 'annus,' that is, year, and ka, Ma, Ga for rising powers of it. (kilo-annus, Mega-annus, Giga-annus) The task force really downplayed the importance of differentiating between geohistorical dates and spans of time (again, not enough buy-in from earth sciences!) and suggested that the new convention could be used interchangeably for both.

But then you get issues like this, where my usage of 3,000 Ma isn't discussing a quantity of time, but pointing at a specific time, which is more appropriate in this context. And confusion results.

TL;DR- It isn't that I'm not using SI standard for a given quantity, it's that very recently an SI standard was put into place that uses an abbreviation that already had a well-established use. In this context 3,000 Ma doesn't refer to a quantity of time, but to a specific point in time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, after reading up on this I think I see my confusion.

Apparently, geologists have indeed historically used not only mya and bya, but also Ma to mean "million years ago" as you say, and Ga for "billion years ago". Not for "Million years ago" though (which would be a weird use of capitalization for million) but because Ma is the SI unit megaannum and geologists are too lazy to write "ago".

And apparently, in modern times, geologists have generally gone with the ones without the y.

Which pisses off SI unit authorities, since Ma/Ga just mean megaannum and gigaannum to them, and geologists wiriting Ma/Ga to mean "Ma ago" or "Ga ago" is a misuse of units according to them.

And I understand why, because as a non-geologist, mya/bya are intuitively understandable, and "Ma/Ga ago" would be intuitively understandable too, while even if I already knew the a in Ma/Ga stands for annum it would not tell me that there's an "ago" anywhere in there (unless I were a geologist). In fact, since Ma/Ga mean "Ma/Ga ago" to geologists, apparently they have to come up with different non-SI units when they don't mean "ago".

So I guess, in geology, writing mya would be outdated, but the SI standard would be "Ma ago", and if that sounds clunky to you I'd at least put in a footnote saying Ma means "million years ago" in geologist-speak.

Note: astronomy and paleontology still use mya/bya… only geophysics has come up with their own units :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good eye on that extra 'were.' Dunno how I missed that one. :P

In geology parlance Ma does stand for "Millions of years ago" but it's used more to refer to a specific point in time rather than describe how old something is. It's the difference between saying 'This happened in 1000 AD' or 'This happened a thousand years ago.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I removed the second were in "were the field were not in place". If you prefer to keep that were, and make it "if the field were not in place" go ahead, but seems you had a combination of the 2 going :p

(~3,000 Ma)

Is this supposed to be mya? As far as I can tell, 3000 Ma just means 3 billion years, while 3000 mya means 3 billion years ago which actually refers to a time period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heyo, I remember this. It's a neat piece and got a good sci-fi feel with the narrative progression. Here's a +1 from me. Nicely done, author!

item.title: Good, but maybe a little too technical
item.content:encoded:
First and most importantly I like this SCP. It's an interesting and well fleshed out idea, and the mystery of seeing what you could learn after the proper shielding is created is great.

With that out of the way I have a few suggestions I think would make it better.

The first is I think it's a bit too technical. A good example being int the last addendum: "a member of the family Brontotheriidae". I had no idea what a Brontotheriidae was and even after I looked it up it didn't actually contribute much to the story. You could just have easily said the tracks were from a woolly mammoth and functionally nothing changes it just keeps the layman reader from getting lost.

My second suggestion would be to do a bit of changes to the description section. It's technical
enough and long enough I was having a bit of trouble getting through it. I had to reread several parts and I still think I missed some things. What I would suggest doing is one of two things. Either make a test log of the experiments where you learn about the properties (you already made mention of a test with rolling balls into the field), or pepper in the technical talk with a bit of simply speak. So for example: "During recovery agents described it as XXX, but this is technically inaccurate because XXX". I think adding either or both of those things would make the description section much more readable and enjoyable.

My last suggestion is small but I'd hint towards the existence of SCP-3364-01 much earlier, potentially in the special containment procedure. I'd argue that SCP-3364-01 is easily the best part of the SCP and planting the suspicion early to get the reader asking questions would in my opinion greatly improve the experience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, here we go, my first bona fide attempt at an SCP. Thanks to Zyn for helping me get the basic concept and the draft punched into shape, and the folks at #Site-19 as well.
